headlines.title: 2 stabbed, multiple arrested after Mexico World Cup watch party in San Jose
headlines.description: Two people were stabbed and multiple others were arrested after a World Cup watch party in downtown San Jose where thousands gathered to watch Mexico defeat Ecuador 2-0 and advance to the Round of 16 of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, police said. Excitement had overflowed in San Pedro Square as Mexico achieved a historic victory at Mexico City Stadium. “I feel very proud… my country… the people… I have no words,” one fan said. Another fan said, “Feels amazing. We’re the underdogs right now… we’re making history.” Organizers said about 30,000 fans packed San Pedro Square, where four giant screens were set up for the World Cup matches. The celebration became rowdy after the game ended. Just before midnight, San Jose police ordered people to disperse outside San Pedro Square. Video shot near Santa Clara Street and Market Street showed officers in riot gear lining the streets. Police said multiple people were arrested and booked into the Santa Clara County Main Jail on various charges. In a statement, San Jose police said the overwhelming majority of attendees celebrated responsibly, but numerous people engaged in disorderly and dangerous behavior as the night went on. Officers responded to multiple fights, fireworks being set off in crowded areas and other incidents. Police said two separate altercations resulted in stabbings. The victims suffered injuries that were not considered life-threatening. Police also said officers responded after a crowd surrounded an ambulance and several people climbed onto the vehicle, interfering with emergency responders. Authorities said members of the crowd threw bottles at officers as they worked to restore order.

headlines.title: Amber Alert issued after baby abducted in Hayward
headlines.description: California authorities issued early Wednesday morning an Amber Alert for a 3-month-old Hayward infant believed to have been abducted, urging residents across parts of Northern and Southern California to watch for the child, the suspected abductor and their vehicle. The California Highway Patrol issued the alert on behalf of the Hayward Police Department for Alameda, San Joaquin, Kern and Los Angeles counties. The infant, Maxence Sirois, was last seen with her alleged abductor at about 1 a.m. Sunday near McBride Lane and Underwood Avenue in Hayward, the CHP said. Police identified the suspected abductor as 42-year-old Marina Kazakova. Kazakova is believed to be driving a white 2016 Mazda CX-5 with California license plate 7SJW989. The infant is described as about 2 feet tall, weighing 16 pounds, with blue eyes and no hair. Kazakova is described as 5 feet 6 inches tall, weighing about 128 pounds, with brown hair and brown eyes. She was last seen wearing a black shirt and blue jeans. Anyone who sees the child, the suspect or the vehicle is urged to call 911 immediately, the CHP said. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Fan calls “bulls***” on StubHub ‘upgrade' that moved World Cup seat 23 rows back
headlines.description: Several viewers tell us they’re having to play serious offense just to keep their pricey seats for this week’s USA-Bosnia World Cup game in Santa Clara. Sanjay Kalra in Saratoga, Matthew Gaubatz in San Francisco, and Cristina Molina in the South Bay tell us they are not happy with the ticket re-seller StubHub. “I was so mad,” Kalra said. “Very frustrating,” said Gaubatz. “I’m sorry, this is bull****,” Molina said. Molina and her husband Jeremy — who own a mom-n-pop company called Elite Drywall in Mountain View — paid a pretty penny on StubHub for a prime perch: Premium upper deck tickets to see Team USA play in Santa Clara. “First row,” she said. “And it was like $2,200 bucks for both… Why not? When’s the next World Cup gonna be here?” Then came a surprise note from StubHub. “They’re like, ‘Hey, we upgraded you guys,’” she said. Was it really an upgrade? Cristina and her husband were sitting in row 1 of section 419. Stub Hub moved them to section 411, closer to center field. That’s likely a more expensive section. But Cristina was no longer in row 1, where she wanted to sit. Her supposedly “upgraded” seats were now up in row 24. “I was like: ‘How is that an upgrade?’ Now we’re two rows away from the last row,” she said. Kalra, in Saratoga, says he paid $2,300 on StubHub for five tickets back in November. Suddenly, this past week, the seller backed out. “I was really shocked,” he said. “I feel they are fleecing the customers.” Gaubatz, in San Francisco, spent $2,800 on StubHub in April for three seats together. When his tickets arrived, only two seats were together. “One was completely on the other side of the stadium,” he said. “We just felt so powerless.” Kalra, Molina, and Gaubatz are not alone. News reports around the country feature fans in similar situations. So, what’s going on? StubHub told us users’ issues are “largely transfer problems, not ticket problems.” StubHub accused FIFA’s ticket system of “significant performance issues that have affected transfers across all resale platforms.” FIFA said it, “rejects any suggestion that the functional issues being experienced by… third-party platforms… are the result of FIFAs ticketing infrastructure.” We turned to Brian Hess with the Sports Fan Coalition, a national, non-profit consumer advocacy group. “We’re not hearing a lot of positive things about the ticketing experience for fans of the World Cup,” he said. Hess encourages World Cup ticket buyers to flag issues early. And, stand your ground for replacement seats instead of refunds. “Demand they get you in the door. Fight for that,” Hess said. The fans, Molina, Kalra, and Gaubatz, needed help getting to this week’s game. They all say StubHub exercised its Fan Protect guarantee, which offers replacement seats -or- your money back at its “sole discretion.” They all say StubHub offered a refund. But the numbers don’t balance. In Cristina’s case, a $2,200 refund is not enough to buy new row 1 seats in the upper deck, because prices have skyrocketed. “Literally like $10,000 each. There’s no way we’re gonna get back into the game,” she said. Same math problem for Gaubatz and Kalra. “The price of the ticket had gone 10x,” Kalra said. We checked. And yes, some ticket prices today are up to 10 times more than what people paid months ago. So, these viewers went looking for help. “I just typed the situation into the ChatGPT and it said, ‘OK, Here’s the next thing you need to do: contact NBC,’” Gaubatz said. Cristina and Kalra contacted NBC Bay Area Responds, too. “NBC actually helps consumers,” Kalra said. We then contacted StubHub. Next, it took action. StubHub gave Kalra’s family five replacement seats at no extra cost. “They are really super excited to go see the match now,” he said. Same success for Gaubatz: Three replacement seats — together. “We are GOING,” he said. Finally, what about Molina’s so-called “upgrade” from row 1 to row 24? A StubHub rep specifically said: “We apologize for their poor experience.” StubHub moved Cristina back to row 1. She reached her goal, after asking us for an assist. “It worked, so I’m excited,” Molina said. Recently, California Attorney General Rob Bonta sent FIFA a letter demanding answers about its ticketing practices. So, we wondered if the AG was preparing to mail StubHub and other ticket re-sellers a similar letter. We asked, but the AG’s office did not respond.

headlines.title: Bay Area fans scramble for Team USA gear ahead of historic World Cup match
headlines.description: Team USA World Cup fever is sweeping the Bay Area as fans scramble for merchandise ahead of Wednesday’s Round of 32 match against Bosnia-Herzegovina. The highly anticipated knockout game at the San Francisco Bay Area Stadium in Santa Clara marks only the second time the region has hosted a U.S. World Cup match. Satyan Raju and his friend of more than 35 years, Manoj Panda, both of San Ramon, are among the lucky few who secured tickets to the historic match. “Absolutely a home game,” Panda said. “Soccer and football is very popular. We grew up playing, and it’s good to see. We’ve been watching on television for a long time, but this is the first time I’m watching in the stadium.” For Raju, Wednesday will be a World Cup homecoming. He was in the stands 32 years ago when the U.S. played in the Bay Area’s only other domestic World Cup game — a 1-0 loss to eventual champion Brazil in the 1994 Round of 16 at Stanford Stadium. “I went to the Brazil game, and it was in Stanford Stadium, and I’m looking forward to this game,” Raju said. While he admits he doesn’t remember many details from the 1994 match other than the excitement of the crowd, Raju said he and his three friends are ready to represent the U.S. this time around. After a desperate search across the Bay Area, the group finally found the Team USA jerseys they had been looking for at a pop-up store in Livermore. “I’m very excited,” Raju said. “We went to a lot of other shops, like Dick’s Sporting Goods, and everywhere is sold out.” Jennifer Shipe, the manager of the FIFA World Cup pop-up store at the Livermore Premium Outlets, said the location is constantly pulling inventory from the back as U.S. apparel flies off the shelves. “We’ve actually restocked throughout the whole entire day,” Shipe said. “We don’t want any leftover space in between.” Shipe noted that fans are buying in bulk, pointing out one family that purchased more than 15 jerseys and official uniforms just before Raju arrived. With merchandise selling out across the region, Wednesday’s crowd is expected to be a sea of red, white and blue. Raju and his friends plan to be right in the middle of it, raising the decibel level for the home team. “We’re looking forward to it,” Raju said. “Big party tomorrow at Levi’s.”

headlines.title: Head of San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce resigns amid scandalous legal disputes
headlines.description: President of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce Donald Luu has resigned from his influential position as ongoing legal battles embroil one of the oldest Chinese American business organizations in the United States. According to Luu’s attorney, his decision to step down partly stems from “unsubstantiated rumors and gossip” that were becoming a distraction for the chamber. I made the difficult decision to resign from my position as President of the Board of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce so that I could focus on my family and not allow an employee’s false allegations against me to distract from the essential work of the chamber. Donald Luu, former president of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce “I made the difficult decision to resign from my position as President of the Board of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce so that I could focus on my family and not allow an employee’s false allegations against me to distract from the essential work of the chamber,” Luu said in a statement to NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit. The chamber, which boasts a storied history spanning more than a century, remains one of San Francisco’s most powerful and influential civic institutions, hosting some of the city’s best-known cultural events, including the Chinese New Year Parade. Donald Luu, president of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce, resigned from his post on June 8, three weeks prior to when his term was set to expire. Donald Luu resigned three weeks before his term was set to expire Luu’s term as president was set to expire Tuesday, but the longtime activist and community organizer resigned more than three weeks early. Luu said he remains an active member of the chamber’s board of directors. This all comes amid accusations from a chamber employee, Judy Lee. According to a statement from her attorney, Lee is alleging sexual harassment, unwelcome advances, and a hostile work environment during her time with the chamber. Ms. Lee was forced to endure years of unwelcome advances and retaliatory behavior, as well as the Chinese Chamber’s refusal to properly investigate or remediate the misconduct, as required both by law and by common decency. Kathleen Alparce, attorney for chamber employee Judy Lee “Ms. Lee voiced her complaints numerous times directly to the Chinese Chamber before taking this step, yet the organization’s leadership took no action to stop the abuse,” her attorney Kathleen Alparce wrote in a statement. “Ms. Lee was forced to endure years of unwelcome advances and retaliatory behavior, as well as the Chinese Chamber’s refusal to properly investigate or remediate the misconduct, as required both by law and by common decency.” Lee, who remains an employee of the chamber, also sits on San Francisco’s Building Inspection Commission, which is responsible for overseeing construction permits and building safety throughout the city. Judy Lee, an employee of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce, is alleging she endured sexual harassment, retaliation, and a hostile work environment. Alparce said for legal reasons her client isn’t saying who exactly at the chamber she is accusing of impropriety. Luu denies any involvement. “Additionally, to protect myself against these inaccurate claims, I have filed legal action against the employee,” Luu said in a statement. Luu’s attorney provided NBC Bay Area with a copy of his suit and a court receipt showing it was officially submitted to the court. The suit accuses Lee of extortion and defamation, saying she “fabricated felony accusations” against him. Luu also accuses Lee of violating a “confidential settlement agreement” they both entered into, alleging Lee tried to weaponize “its very existence.” The lawsuit does not detail what exactly was included in the settlement agreement. AAPI community May 31, 2023 Investigation Feb 19, 2025 Allegations of a hidden camera NBC Bay Area has also learned of a separate lawsuit, filed by three other chamber employees against the chamber itself, which accuses Judy Lee of setting up a hidden camera inside the office. The lawsuit, filed by Lainar Chan, Mavis Ngo, and Christine Hui, accuses the Chinese Chamber of Commerce of “failing to properly monitor, supervise, or control its employee, Judy Lee, allowing her to install an unauthorized recording device with potential audio and video capture capabilities in the workplace.” “Plaintiffs suffered severe emotional distress, anxiety, loss of dignity, and a compromised sense of safety,” the lawsuit states. The complaint does not provide a reason as to why Lee would allegedly install a hidden camera inside the chamber office. Lee, through her attorney, denied the accusations and said the chamber failed to take appropriate action after Lee made numerous complaints about a hostile work environment. Ms. Lee had to take her personal safety and well-being into her own hands. Kathleen Alparce, attorney for chamber employee Judy Lee “In fact, instead of recognizing or acknowledging Ms. Lee as a victim, the Chinese Chamber and its leadership chose to villainize Ms. Lee,” said Alparce. “Ms. Lee had to take her personal safety and well-being into her own hands.” NBC Bay Area asked Alparce what was meant by saying Lee took matters “into her own hands,” and asked if that statement had anything to do with the allegation that Lee set up a hidden camera inside the chamber. “Based on ongoing legal matters and other legal considerations, we are unable to comment of provide additional information at this time,” she said. Contact The Investigative Unit submit tips | 1-888-996-TIPS | e-mail Bigad Follow @BigadShaban on social media Bigad Shaban

headlines.title: Alameda County supervisors unanimously approve policy-driven reparations plan for Black residents
headlines.description: After nearly three years of research and community engagement, the Alameda County Board of Supervisors unanimously passed a sweeping set of reparations recommendations Tuesday aimed at confronting the county’s history of racial inequity. While reparations are often associated with direct cash payments, the Alameda County Reparations Commission’s plan takes a different approach. The 43 phased recommendations task the board with implementing policies designed to create lasting institutional change rather than issuing individual checks. The commission is now calling on county leaders to put the recommendations into action. Initial steps include creating a permanent committee and launching an equity assessment. The plan’s immediate priorities focus heavily on housing and economic justice, including closing the wealth gap through support for Black-owned businesses, offering down payment assistance, and addressing the ongoing impacts of redlining and displacement. It also calls for targeted investments in Black youth and mental health. Long-term goals include restitution for wrongful convictions, broader criminal justice reform and expanding access to education. “Our expectation is the opportunities that exist for you are the same for everybody else—the ability to buy a home, to get the job, to get the training, to get healthcare,” said Debra Gore, chair of the Alameda County Reparations Commission. Gore emphasized the importance of local government taking accountability. “It’s shifting the way that it looks at the harms it does and the repairs to make,” Gore said. “That shift is overdue. It has to be done at the county level.” Alameda County Supervisor Nate Miley, who spearheaded the commission with support from Supervisor Elisa Márquez, said the plan provides a critical foundation for future legislation. “These collective efforts produce research, recommendations and a framework for repair that will inform county policy and contribute to ongoing efforts to remedy systemic inequities and improve outcomes,” Miley said. The recommendations were shaped by data and community listening sessions, including forums hosted by the Black Cultural Zone. Supporters said the plan needed to be rooted in resident input and driven by those with lived experiences of racial inequity. Carolyn Johnson, CEO of the Black Cultural Zone, said officially adopting the recommendations is a crucial step toward accountability. “When you do this, no one can forget, you can’t wipe it away, you can’t hide it,” Johnson said. “It is on the record, and the efforts that we all believe are necessary are on the record, and I believe that’s the most important thing.” Although the adopted plan currently has no set price tag, advocates at Tuesday’s meeting stressed that true healing will require a tangible, well-funded commitment from the county, declaring that the time for study has concluded and the era of active repair has begun.

headlines.title: San Francisco community picks up pieces, seeks cause after devastating church fire
headlines.description: Members of a historic church in San Francisco’s Pacific Heights neighborhood are picking up the pieces following a devastating fire that shattered stained-glass windows, destroyed the roof and caused the partial collapse of an exterior wall. More than 100 firefighters responded to the blaze at the Seventh-day Adventist church, a neighborhood landmark since the 1890s. San Francisco Fire Chief Dean Crispen said the church’s size and age created special challenges for crews. “Some of them have never been to a fire of this magnitude before,” Crispen said. “So we did learn a lot about the capabilities of our auxiliary water supply system. We were able to pull water from a high-pressure water system.” Crispen noted the high-pressure nozzles sprayed brown water due to sediment in the system’s reservoirs and tanks. Fire units remained on the scene the following day to monitor for flare-ups and assess the structural integrity of the collapsed wall. Investigators are still working to determine the cause of the fire, including whether it was connected to a renovation project underway at the church. The fire also caused collateral damage to the building next door, displacing residents and temporarily shuttering Eliza’s Restaurant, a family business that opened about 35 years ago. The restaurant owners, who live in an apartment above the business, safely evacuated with an infant but sustained significant water damage to their home. Crispen toured the damage with Emily, a family member of the restaurant’s owners. “We’re definitely closed this entire week, but once we are open back up please, we encourage you to come,” Emily said. “Send them some love. This is a family business, it’s all my uncles and aunts, my parents.” The church’s lead pastor spent much of the day on site assessing the ruins. According to a prepared statement, the congregation will join a nearby Seventh-day Adventist church for its July 4 services.

headlines.title: USMNT players sign autographs outside team hotel in San Jose ahead of World Cup match
headlines.description: Fans of the U.S. men’s national team gathered in downtown San Jose Tuesday to catch a glimpse of the players ahead of Wednesday’s knockout round World Cup match in Santa Clara. Dozens of fans, some holding pens, posters, and shirts, lined up outside the Marriott hotel, waiting for hours to see the players in person. When the team bus pulled up and the players filed out, the fans went into a frenzy. Some players stopped to sign autographs. Defender Mark McKenzie even allowed 11-year-old Genesis Samarron’s pet bird Mongo walk up his arm. “Mongo got to go on some of the players,” Samarron said. “They were happy. They met Mongo and they got to hold him and pet him. They loved him.” NBC Bay Area’s Marianne Favro has more in the video report above. World Cup 18 hours ago San Jose Jun 29

headlines.title: Oakland man faces life in prison after guilty verdict in torture, murder of partner
headlines.description: An Oakland man is facing the possibility of life in prison after he was found guilty of killing a 25-year-old woman during a brutal 2024 domestic violence attack that drew murder and torture charges. An Alameda County jury found Victor Frieson, 30, guilty of second-degree murder, torture, and corporal injury to a romantic partner with great bodily injury for killing Zoe Nika Reidy Watts in Oakland on March 1, 2024, the Alameda County District Attorney’s Office announced on Tuesday. Prosecutors say the pair had an “on-again, off-again” relationship for less than a year but during that short time, there were two reported domestic violence attacks, one in San Francisco and one in Oakland. During the trial, evidence was presented that showed Frieson beat Watts to death inside his Oakland apartment. Alameda County District Attorney Ursula Jones Dickson said the attack that killed Watts, a beloved San Francisco State University student and artist, was “an act of extraordinary violence. “She was a talented artist, a dedicated student, and a young woman whose future was filled with promise,” Jones Dickson said. “While no verdict can restore what her family, friends, and the Bay Area artistic community have lost, we hope today’s decision provides a measure of accountability and peace.” Frieson was arrested a few days after the killing and held without bail in Santa Rita Jail. He was also being held for allegedly failing to register as a sex offender and is scheduled for sentencing on Sept. 17 in Alameda County Superior Court in Oakland.

headlines.title: Wong Kim Ark's descendants celebrate Supreme Court's birthright citizenship decision
headlines.description: The 1898 U.S. Supreme Court case of Wong Kim Ark, a cook born in San Francisco’s Chinatown, was a landmark decision that affirmed the right to citizenship for those born on U.S. soil. Nearly 130 years later, Wong’s descendants celebrated Tuesday’s Supreme Court decision that upheld his win for birthright citizenship. “He stood up for the rights of all of us Americans — it just so happens that I am related to him,” said Wong’s great grandson, Norman Wong, in a statement. “Today’s ruling shows that his victory remains as important now as it was in 1898.” “I’m relieved, as I’m sure many others are, because this decision was really important,” said Wong’s great granddaughter, Sandra Wong, in an interview. Norman and Sandra were born and raised in San Francisco — the same city where their great grandfather created a life for himself as a cook. However, Wong Kim Ark was denied entry back into San Francisco after returning from a trip to China despite being born on U.S. soil. Wong Kim Ark fought in the courts and his case went all the way to the Supreme Court, where he ultimately won. The case set a landmark precedent declaring birthright citizenship. “My great grandfather, Wong Kim Ark, never set out to become a symbol,” Norman Wong said. “He was one man, only a cook, and yet he stood up for what was right, and I believe that it has made a difference.” But when President Donald Trump challenged birthright citizenship at the beginning of his second term by issuing an executive order, Sandra and Norman Wong realized that their great grandfather’s fight was not over. “I never really thought this could happen again, that it would be going before the Supreme Court,” Sandra Wong said. Supreme Court 18 hours ago Supreme Court Jun 30 When oral arguments were heard in the Supreme Court back in April, Norman traveled to Washington, D.C., to rally and share details about his great grandfather’s legacy. “His fight was not just his own, it was for me and for generations to come,” Norman Wong said in a speech at the rally. “This is Wong Kim Ark’s legacy. This is my legacy. It is our legacy, and now it is our responsibility to protect it for our children and for generations yet to come.” The anticipation leading up to the decision started to sink in for Sandra on Friday when a mural of Wong Kim Ark was unveiled in Chinatown. “It’s been a bit of a nerve-wracking time over the last couple days,” she said. In a 6-3 decision announced Tuesday, the Supreme Court ruled against Trump’s challenge and upheld birthright citizenship in the 14th Amendment of the U.S. Constitution, reaffirming its original decision in Wong Kim Ark’s 1898 case. Other local and state leaders weighed in on the decision, with some highlighting Wong Kim Ark’s legacy. “Birthright citizenship is as clear cut as legal principles come, and its story is deeply rooted in San Francisco history,” said San Francisco City Attorney David Chiu in a statement. “As a birthright citizen, I know my place in this country is possible because of the courage of Wong Kim Ark and immigrants like my parents.” “Today’s ruling preserved a promise that has defined America for generations and been a cornerstone of democracy,” said Gov. Gavin Newsom in a statement. “The Constitution prevailed. We have all prevailed.”
headlines.source: Jodi Hernandez and Alise Maripuu | Bay City News

headlines.title: U.S. set for Round of 32 World Cup game with Bosnia-Herzegovina as knockout play begins
headlines.description: The U.S. team spent Monday like millions of other interested followers of the World Cup, watching thrilling games from other venues that included a major upset with Germany falling to Paraguay, three games that went down to the wire and two nervy shootouts. Then the reality quickly set in that they would soon be back in the spotlight themselves, with a round of 32 match against Bosnia-Herzegovina on Wednesday that will go a long way to determining how successful this World Cup will be. “In the moment you’re a fan, and then afterward you realize that this is also the same tournament that we’re playing. I think it’s a weird dynamic,” U.S. defender Chris Richards said Tuesday before the team’s final practice ahead of the knockout round. “But we saw an upset yesterday, so it’s making sure that we don’t allow that to happen to us.” After starting the tournament with convincing wins over Paraguay and Australia that clinched the top spot in the group before a meaningless loss to Turkey last week, the Americans enter this knockout round as the favorites to advance for a change against a Bosnian team that has never made it past the group stage before this year. US has only one World Cup knockout round win ever But history is against the team as the U.S. men have had little success in the past in the knockout round of the World Cup or against European opposition. While the Americans made it to the semifinals at the inaugural tournament in 1930 by winning their group, their only win ever in a knockout round came in 2002 when they beat rival Mexico 2-0 in South Korea before losing to Germany in the quarterfinals. In the eight other tournaments since the U.S. returned to the World Cup in 1990 for the first time in 40 years, the U.S. lost its first knockout game — the round of 16 instead of the round of 32 — four times, failed to make it out of the group three times and failed even to qualify in 2018. Christian Pulisic said the approach doesn’t change in this round and that mood around the team remains light even with the high stakes in this part of the tournament. Pulisic said he’s healthy and ready to start after missing one game with a calf injury and playing the last game as a second-half substitute. “You just want to extend it as long as you can, because some of the best memories I have in my life was last World Cup in Qatar,” Pulisic said. “Now being here, it’s just special to be here. You just don’t want it to end. So there’s definitely a lot of enthusiasm within the team.” World Cup 22 hours ago World Cup 23 hours ago US has struggled against European teams The U.S. lost its knockout game four years ago to the Netherlands 3-1. That came after a 2-1 loss to Belgium in 2014 and is part of a long run of futility against European teams. The U.S. is 1-13-7 against European teams at the World Cup since 1990, with the only win coming in 2002 in the opener against Portugal. The Americans are winless in their last 13 matches against European squads at the tournament since then. While Bosnia, 61st in the FIFA rankings, lacks the pedigree of many of those past European opponents, the Americans know this matchup won’t be easy. The Bosnians secured a spot in the World Cup for the second time ever by winning penalty shootouts against Wales and four-time World Cup champion Italy in the European playoffs and then advanced out of the group thanks to a draw in Toronto against host Canada and a 3-1 win over Qatar. “Every team brings different challenges,” Richards said. “Ultimately the Bosnia team, they were good defensively and good going forward. It’s making sure that we take care of business on both ends. They have a very experienced attack so it’s making sure we do our best in the back to take that away from them.” With a victory on Wednesday, the U.S. team would advance to the round of 16 next Monday in Seattle against the winner of Wednesday’s game between Belgium and Senegal. It only gets tougher after that with powerhouses like Spain still alive in their half of the bracket, but the results so far in this tournament with teams like Germany and the Netherlands getting knocked out early show what’s possible. “It’s our chance to go farther in this tournament,” Richards said. “We’ve seen two big boys fall. So it’s just a matter of us getting through this game and putting in a good performance and hopefully we continue that. It’ll start to open up. So it is very exciting for us.”
headlines.source: Ali Reid and Josh Dubow | The Associated Press

headlines.title: San Jose erupts as Mexico wins first World Cup knockout match in 40 years
headlines.description: An estimated 30,000 soccer fans packed into downtown San Jose’s San Pedro Square, erupting into celebration as Mexico won its first World Cup knockout match in 40 years to advance to the Round of 16. A sea of green jerseys filled the plaza, spilling into surrounding streets and parking garages as the viewing area reached capacity two hours before kickoff. When the final whistle blew, the massive crowd celebrated a historic victory that many believe is just the beginning of a deep tournament run. Mexico secured its ticket to the next round by scoring twice in the first half, with each goal sending the watch party into a frenzy. “I feel very proud — my country, the people. I have no words,” one fan said. “Feels amazing,” another fan added. “We’re the underdogs right now, we’re making history.” 2026 World Cup Jun 28 San Jose Jun 24 2026 World Cup Jun 18 Organizers set up four giant screens for the event. With the main plaza overflowing, many fans found viewing spots at nearby venues like the restaurant SP2. “Honestly, they’re always over capacity over there, so we’re always open for whoever wants to come over here,” an SP2 representative said. “Business-wise we’ve been doing really well. Everybody’s enjoying it. We’re having a great time.” Security remained highly visible throughout the night. Event staff conducted bag checks at the entrances, police officers directed traffic and paramedics staged nearby to manage the massive crowd. The viewing infrastructure at San Pedro Square is not going anywhere. Organizers expect another massive crowd the following day for a Team USA watch party. Meanwhile, Mexico fans are turning their attention to Sunday, when their team takes the field again in the Round of 16. “Let’s go!” one fan cheered as the plaza celebrated. “We’re winning the World Cup! 2026!”
